prompt: An AI code-review bot reads a pull-request diff. Why might that diff use far more tokens than an equally long block of plain English prose?
choices: Source code is exempt from tokenization
Punctuation, indentation, and identifiers in code each cost tokens, so code tokenizes 'heavier' than prose
Diffs are always counted as a single token
Prose is billed per word while code is billed per line

prompt: Select ALL statements that are true about tokens and the cost of an AI feature in the pipeline.
choices: LLM usage is typically billed per token
Input tokens (the diff/log you send) and output tokens (the AI's reply) are usually billed separately
One source-code line always equals exactly one token
A model's context window limit is measured in tokens, not lines

prompt: A developer needs an AI tool to review a single auto-generated file of 20,000 lines, but the call fails before the model responds. Using token concepts, what is the most likely cause and fix?
choices: The file is fine; the model simply chose not to answer, so retry unchanged
The file is far too many input tokens to fit the model's context window, so chunk or skip it
Output tokens were exhausted, so lower the price per token
The diff had too few tokens, so pad it with whitespace

prompt: A PM is budgeting an AI reviewer. A developer reports each PR averages about 9,300 input tokens and 550 output tokens, the team merges 1,500 PRs a month, and the provider charges different rates for input and output. What should the PM do to produce an accurate monthly estimate?
choices: Add input and output tokens together and apply one price, since all tokens cost the same
Use only input tokens, because the AI's comments are free
Cost input and output tokens separately at their own rates, then multiply each by 1,500 PRs
Ignore token counts and estimate purely from the number of PRs

prompt: Trimming the prompt lowers token cost but risks dropping context the model needs. For a high-stakes legal-summary feature where a missed clause is costly, which *trade-off* is right?
choices: Trim as aggressively as possible to minimize cost on every call
Always send the entire document so nothing is ever omitted
Keep the context the task genuinely needs even at higher token cost; trim only clearly-irrelevant text
Switch to the cheapest model so token price stops mattering
